findings.summary: performance.py shim re-exports from two sub-modules but is itself still imported by tests, creating a permanent indirection layer with no documented retirement plan
evidence: performance.py docstring says 'Import from those modules directly for new code' but the two test files still import exclusively from infrastructure.core.performance, not from the canonical split modules
The shim's main() CLI entry point creates a call-site dependency on the shim module itself, making dead removal harder
findings.suggestion: Update the two test files to import from infrastructure.core.stage_monitor and infrastructure.core.function_profiler directly, then delete performance.py or reduce it to a one-line deprecation warning; document a version at which it will be removed

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/__init__.py mixes a guarded optional import (dashboard_generator) inline with unguarded imports, creating asymmetric failure modes at package import time
evidence: Lines 11-20: dashboard_generator is wrapped in try/except ImportError and sets _DASHBOARD_AVAILABLE = True/False, but the flag is never re-exported or checked anywhere visible in __init__.py
All other sub-module imports (error_aggregator, executive_reporter, output_reporter, pipeline_reporter, etc.) are unconditional — a failure in any of them raises at import time with no fallback
The asymmetry means callers cannot reliably reason about which symbols are available at import time vs. conditionally
findings.suggestion: Either guard all optional sub-modules consistently with try/except and expose availability flags, or move dashboard_generator to a lazy import inside the function that uses it and remove the inline guard from __init__.py

findings.summary: stage_monitor imports from logging_utils which in turn imports from logging_progress and logging_helpers, creating a deep cross-cutting chain through the core layer
evidence: stage_monitor.py line 19: 'from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import format_duration, get_logger'
logging_utils.py lines 24-28 imports from logging_formatters, logging_helpers, logging_progress, _optional_deps, logging_constants — five internal imports
function_profiler.py line 20 also imports from logging_utils, so both performance sub-modules share this same deep chain
Any circular addition to logging_utils (e.g., importing from stage_monitor for resource logging) would create a cycle
findings.suggestion: stage_monitor and function_profiler should import format_duration from infrastructure.core.logging_helpers directly (it has no further internal imports) rather than routing through logging_utils

findings.summary: log_stage_with_eta is defined in both logging_utils.py and logging_progress.py with different signatures and different behaviour
evidence: logging_utils.py line 357: def log_stage_with_eta(stage_num, total_stages, stage_name, pipeline_start=None, logger=None) — takes pipeline stage numbers and an optional wall-clock start time
logging_progress.py line 431: def log_stage_with_eta(stage, current, total, elapsed_time, logger=None) — takes a stage name string and a pre-computed elapsed float

logging_utils.__all__ includes 'log_stage_with_eta' (line 430 area); logging_progress also exports the same name — a caller importing both modules gets two different functions under the same name with no indication of which is canonical
findings.suggestion: Remove one definition; the logging_progress version is the lower-level utility (takes elapsed directly) and belongs there. The logging_utils version, which depends on calculate_eta from logging_progress, should either be removed or renamed to log_pipeline_stage_with_eta to distinguish it from the lower-level variant

findings.summary: log_resource_usage is defined in both logging_utils.py and logging_progress.py with incompatible signatures
evidence: logging_utils.py line 386: def log_resource_usage(stage_name='', logger=None) — calls psutil internally to fetch live resource data
logging_progress.py line 458: def log_resource_usage(cpu_percent=None, memory_mb=None, logger=None) — takes pre-computed values as parameters
logging_utils.__all__ exports 'log_resource_usage'; logging_progress also defines it, so the two modules expose conflicting contracts under the same public name
findings.suggestion: Consolidate: keep only the logging_progress variant (caller-supplied values, no psutil dependency) and remove the logging_utils variant; if psutil-fetching is needed, create a distinctly-named function like log_live_resource_usage in logging_utils

findings.summary: generate_markdown_report is defined in both pipeline_reporter.py and suite_summary_generator.py with different type signatures; the reporting __init__.py re-exports only the suite_summary_generator version, silently shadowing the pipeline_reporter version
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py line 163: def generate_markdown_report(report: PipelineReport) -> str
suite_summary_generator.py line 248: def generate_markdown_report(data: Dict[str, Any]) -> str
reporting/__init__.py imports generate_markdown_report from suite_summary_generator (line 49-53) and places it in __all__ (line 79)

pipeline_reporter.py line 140 calls its own generate_markdown_report internally, so the function is live but unexported — a caller doing 'from infrastructure.reporting import generate_markdown_report' gets the suite_summary_generator version with no indication the other exists
findings.suggestion: Rename to make purpose explicit: pipeline_reporter.py should use a module-private _generate_pipeline_markdown helper (already only called internally); suite_summary_generator.py's public function should be renamed generate_suite_markdown_report to avoid collision

findings.summary: progress.py mixes three distinct concerns: general-purpose text progress bars (ProgressBar), LLM-specific token tracking (LLMProgressTracker), and multi-substage pipeline tracking (SubStageProgress)
evidence: ProgressBar is a generic terminal UI component
LLMProgressTracker (line 137) is explicitly LLM-domain-specific, tracking tokens-per-second throughput and referencing LLM generation vocabulary
SubStageProgress (line 244) is pipeline-orchestration-specific, calling logger.info with pipeline-stage log lines
LLMProgressTracker belongs in infrastructure/llm/ not infrastructure/core/
findings.suggestion: Move LLMProgressTracker to infrastructure/llm/ (e.g., infrastructure/llm/utils/progress.py). Keep ProgressBar and SubStageProgress in core/progress.py as they are pipeline-generic

findings.summary: logging_progress.py defines three separate ETA calculation functions (calculate_eta, calculate_eta_ema, calculate_eta_with_confidence) where only calculate_eta is actually used by the logging-layer callers; the other two exist purely for the progress.py ProgressBar and SubStageProgress classes
evidence: logging_utils.py only imports calculate_eta from logging_progress (line 26)
progress.py imports all three: calculate_eta, calculate_eta_ema, calculate_eta_with_confidence (lines 17-20)
The EMA and confidence-interval functions have no logging concern; they are pure math utilities living in a logging-named module
findings.suggestion: Extract calculate_eta, calculate_eta_ema, and calculate_eta_with_confidence to a standalone infrastructure/core/eta_utils.py module with no logging imports; have both logging_progress.py and progress.py import from there

findings.summary: All five production dependencies in pyproject.toml use lower-bound-only version constraints with no upper bound, leaving the package open to breaking changes from major version upgrades
evidence: numpy>=1.22 — numpy 2.x introduced breaking API changes
matplotlib>=3.7, pypdf>=5.0, pyyaml>=6.0, requests>=2.31.0 — all uncapped

uv.lock exists (implied by git status) so reproducibility is handled at the lock level, but the declared constraints allow a fresh install on a new machine to pull incompatible future majors if the lock is not used
findings.suggestion: Add compatible-release upper bounds (e.g., numpy>=1.22,<3) or use ~= version specifiers (e.g., numpy~=1.22) to prevent silent major-version breakage when the lock file is regenerated

findings.summary: numpy and matplotlib are production dependencies consumed by infrastructure/scientific/ modules that are not optional, creating heavyweight mandatory deps for a 'generic infrastructure' layer
evidence: pyproject.toml lists numpy>=1.22 and matplotlib>=3.7 as unconditional [project].dependencies
infrastructure/scientific/templates.py imports numpy and matplotlib directly (not lazily) at lines 40, 134, 241-242
psutil by contrast is correctly placed in an optional [dependency-groups].monitoring group with graceful fallback via _optional_deps.py
A project that uses only the PDF rendering or validation infrastructure is forced to install numpy and matplotlib
findings.suggestion: Move numpy and matplotlib to an optional [dependency-groups].scientific group and apply the same lazy-import / graceful-degradation pattern already used for psutil; update infrastructure/scientific/templates.py to guard imports

findings.summary: The logging concern is split across five files (logging_utils.py, logging_constants.py, logging_helpers.py, logging_formatters.py, logging_progress.py) creating navigation overhead disproportionate to the complexity of each file
evidence: logging_constants.py is 31 lines — three constants and two feature flags
logging_helpers.py is 55 lines — two functions
logging_formatters.py is 81 lines — two formatter classes
All three are too small to justify a separate file; they exist solely to avoid circular imports within logging_utils.py, which is itself a design symptom
The split was motivated by internal circular-import avoidance (logging_constants docstring: 'avoiding duplicate definitions and circular import issues') rather than independent reusability
findings.suggestion: Consolidate logging_constants.py, logging_helpers.py, and logging_formatters.py back into logging_utils.py; resolve the circular import by reordering definitions within a single file (constants first, then formatters, then high-level utilities). Reserve separate files for logging_progress.py which is substantively large (481 lines) and independently useful

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ contains modules that are not generic infrastructure: menu.py (interactive CLI menus), security.py (credential scanning), credentials.py, and steganography/ exists as a sibling sub-package to core/
evidence: ls output shows menu.py, security.py, credentials.py alongside pipeline.py, checkpoint.py, config_loader.py
menu.py is an interactive shell menu — a UI concern, not a core infrastructure primitive
security.py contains 28 references to numpy/matplotlib/requests per grep output — suggesting non-trivial cross-domain logic inside a 'core' module
CLAUDE.md describes infrastructure/core/ as 'Core utilities (logging, config, exceptions, file operations, pipeline)' — menu and security fall outside this stated scope
findings.suggestion: Move menu.py to infrastructure/cli/ or scripts/; move security.py and credentials.py to infrastructure/security/; this keeps core/ to its documented purpose of logging, config, exceptions, file operations, and pipeline primitives

findings.summary: Multi-paragraph docstrings with Args/Returns blocks on trivially thin functions
evidence: save_test_results (pipeline_reporter.py:439) has a full Args/Returns docstring but is a 3-line open+dump call
format_file_context (exceptions.py:487) has a 14-line docstring + example for a 2-line dict builder
raise_with_context (exceptions.py:461) has a 16-line docstring with a usage example for a 1-line raise wrapper
chain_exceptions (exceptions.py:508) full docstring with example for a 6-line exception chainer
generate_performance_report (pipeline_reporter.py:506) full docstring for a 4-line json.dump wrapper
findings.suggestion: Trim docstrings on trivially obvious helpers to a single-line description; reserve Args/Returns blocks for non-obvious parameters or return shapes.

findings.summary: Leaf exception subclasses with nothing but `pass` add nominal hierarchy without real behaviour
evidence: MissingConfigurationError, InvalidConfigurationError, MarkdownValidationError, PDFValidationError, DataValidationError, CompilationError, ScriptExecutionError, PipelineError, FileOperationError, NotADirectoryError, InvalidFileFormatError, IntegrationError, LiteratureSearchError, APIRateLimitError, InvalidQueryError, LLMConnectionError, LLMTemplateError, ContextLimitError, FormatError, TemplateRenderingError, PublishingError, UploadError, MetadataError, VersionMismatchError, InsufficientCoverageError — all body is `pass`
25 of 32 exception classes carry zero behaviour beyond the parent, adding 100+ lines of structural boilerplate
findings.suggestion: Collapse leaf `pass` siblings that share the same parent and have no site-specific catch differences into two or three semantically distinct parents; only split when callers actually need to discriminate.

findings.summary: generate_translation inlines the full heartbeat/streaming block instead of calling _stream_with_heartbeat
evidence: _stream_with_heartbeat (line 527) exists precisely to encapsulate StreamingProgress + StreamHeartbeatMonitor + streaming loop + fallback
generate_translation (line 739) duplicates all of that verbatim: creates its own StreamingProgress, StreamHeartbeatMonitor, streaming loop (lines 764-796), and fallback (line 797-799)
Adds nested try/except (outer line 762, inner line 783) that _stream_with_heartbeat already handles in one block
findings.suggestion: Replace the inline streaming block in generate_translation with a call to _stream_with_heartbeat, matching the pattern used by generate_review_with_metrics.

findings.summary: performance.py shim adds an unrelated CLI entry point on top of re-exports
evidence: performance.py is documented as a backward-compatible re-export shim (lines 1-10)
It then adds a main() CLI entry point (lines 47-67) that belongs in neither the shim nor the re-exported modules
The CLI duplicates get_performance_monitor()/generate_performance_report() already available via the real modules
findings.suggestion: Move main() to function_profiler.py or a dedicated CLI module; leave the shim as pure re-exports.

findings.summary: generator.py defines a local log_stage() that shadows the same name imported from logging_utils
evidence: logging_utils exports log_stage (logging_utils.py:326)
generator.py defines its own log_stage (line 330): `def log_stage(message: str) -> None: logger.info(f'\n  {message}')`
This is behaviorally identical to log_substep in logging_utils (line 341), creating two names for the same concept across sibling files
findings.suggestion: Remove the local log_stage definition and import log_substep (or log_stage) from logging_utils to keep naming consistent across the infrastructure.

findings.summary: Old-style typing imports (Dict, List, Optional, Tuple, Union) mixed with modern built-in generics in the same codebase
evidence: function_profiler.py line 18: `from typing import Any, Callable, Dict, List, Optional, Union` — uses Dict, List throughout
stage_monitor.py uses `dict[str, Any]`, `list[dict]` (modern built-ins, no capital imports)
generator.py line 9: `from typing import Any, Dict, List, Optional, Tuple` — uses Dict, List throughout
health_check.py uses `dict[str, Any]` built-in style
integrity.py uses `Dict, List` from typing alongside `from __future__ import annotations`
findings.suggestion: Standardise on Python 3.10+ built-in generics (`dict`, `list`, `tuple`) since `from __future__ import annotations` is present in all files; remove the capital-letter typing imports.

findings.summary: Module-level logger is sometimes placed at the top of the file and sometimes after class/function definitions
evidence: stage_monitor.py: `logger = get_logger(__name__)` at line 21 (top, after imports) — consistent
function_profiler.py: `logger = get_logger(__name__)` at line 22 (top, after imports) — consistent
logging_utils.py: no module-level logger defined at all; all functions use `logger = logger or get_logger(__name__)` inline
generator.py: `logger = get_logger(__name__)` at line 63, after 50 lines of imports and after the PROMPT_SYSTEM_AVAILABLE try/except block
findings.suggestion: Place the module-level logger assignment immediately after the import block in every module; use the module-level logger consistently instead of inline fallback patterns.

findings.summary: Some failure paths raise exceptions while equivalent failure paths in sibling functions return None or False
evidence: extract_manuscript_text (generator.py:474): returns (None, metrics) on missing PDF — no exception
stage_monitor.py PerformanceMonitor.stop(): raises BuildError if not started (line 90)
stage_monitor.py StagePerformanceTracker.end_stage(): raises BuildError if start_time is None (line 217)
generate_review_with_metrics (generator.py:642): catches all Exception, returns error string sentinel instead of raising (line 648-651)
warmup_model (generator.py:467): returns (False, 0.0) on Exception — caller cannot distinguish error type
check_ollama_availability (generator.py:334): returns (False, None) on subprocess errors, None model, or missing binary — three distinct error conditions collapsed to the same return value
findings.suggestion: Define and use a Result type or raise domain-specific exceptions (LLMConnectionError, PDFValidationError) consistently; callers should not need to inspect sentinel values to distinguish error kinds.

findings.summary: _stream_with_heartbeat swallows all streaming exceptions silently and falls back to blocking query
evidence: Line 575-577: `except Exception as e: logger.debug(...); return client.query(prompt, options=options)`
Any exception type — timeout, auth failure, model not found, network error — is caught and silently retried as a blocking call at DEBUG level only
The outer caller in generate_review_with_metrics has its own retry loop (line 613), so errors are swallowed twice before surfacing
findings.suggestion: Log at WARNING level at minimum; distinguish transient errors (connection reset) from fatal errors (auth/model-not-found) and re-raise the latter rather than masking them with a blocking fallback.

findings.summary: get_health_status catches all Exception per check and promotes failures to a status string, losing error type
evidence: Lines 55-62: `except Exception as e: logger.warning(...); status['checks'][check_name] = {'status': 'unhealthy', 'error': str(e), ...}`
str(e) is stored — all exception type information (OSError vs ImportError vs AttributeError) is lost
is_healthy() (line 219) returns a bool based on the string 'healthy' — callers cannot distinguish degraded vs fully unhealthy
findings.suggestion: Store `error_type: type(e).__name__` alongside `error` string; have is_healthy() accept a parameter to treat 'degraded' as healthy or unhealthy depending on caller needs.

findings.summary: Several locations use `if len(x) > 0:` or `if not x:` inconsistently when `if x:` suffices
evidence: integrity.py line 192: `if len(lines) > 0:` — idiomatic Python is `if lines:`

stage_monitor.py get_performance_warnings line 264: `avg_duration = sum(durations) / len(durations) if durations else 0` — correct, but line 260 uses `if not self.stages: return warnings` and line 262 accesses self.stages again, making durations conditional redundant

stage_monitor.py get_summary line 312: `average_duration: total_duration / len(self.stages) if self.stages else 0` — the guard `if not self.stages: return {}` at line 303 already guarantees self.stages is non-empty by this point
findings.suggestion: Replace `len(x) > 0` with `x`; remove redundant ternary guards that are already protected by an early-return above.

findings.summary: profile_memory_usage assigns func_result = None then overwrites it unconditionally inside with-block
evidence: Line 233: `func_result = None`
Line 235: `func_result = func(*args, **kwargs)` — the None initialisation is never observed; if func raises the with-block propagates the exception and func_result is never used
findings.suggestion: Remove the `func_result = None` initialisation; assign directly: `with monitor.monitor(...): result = func(...)` and return result.

findings.summary: _is_test_environment performs a three-branch OR check where the third branch is O(n) over all loaded modules
evidence: Lines 36-40: third condition `any('pytest' in str(v) for v in sys.modules.values() if hasattr(v, '__file__'))` iterates every loaded module object on every call
_is_test_environment() is called in both setup_logger() and get_logger(), making the scan happen on every logger creation
The first condition `os.getenv('PYTEST_CURRENT_TEST')` and second `'pytest' in sys.modules` already cover all real pytest invocations; the third is redundant
findings.suggestion: Remove the third branch; cache the result at module level (`_IN_TEST_ENV = bool(os.getenv('PYTEST_CURRENT_TEST') or 'pytest' in sys.modules)`) to avoid repeated iteration.

findings.summary: Hard-coded numeric thresholds appear inline without named constants
evidence: stage_monitor.py line 267: `stage['duration'] > avg_duration * 2` — the 2x multiplier is undocumented magic
stage_monitor.py line 278: `> 1024` (memory MB threshold) inline
stage_monitor.py line 288: `> 90` (CPU percent threshold) inline
health_check.py lines 119, 142: `95.0` used as critical threshold both for memory and disk, but stored as a dict value not a named constant
generator.py line 153: `max(1, int(min_word_count * 0.05))` — the 5% tolerance is undocumented
generator.py line 128: `< 0.5` unique ratio threshold for repetition failure inline
findings.suggestion: Extract thresholds to named module-level constants (e.g. SLOW_STAGE_MULTIPLIER = 2, HIGH_MEMORY_MB = 1024, CRITICAL_RESOURCE_PERCENT = 95.0) with comments explaining the rationale.

findings.summary: Several any([...]) calls wrap a list literal instead of using a generator expression
evidence: Line 209: `has_assessment = any(["clarity" in response_lower, ...])`  — list is fully evaluated before any() short-circuits; generator would short-circuit
Line 239: `has_methodology_content = any(["research design" in response_lower, ...])`  — same pattern
Line 269: `has_recommendations = any(["recommendation" in response_lower, ...])`  — same pattern
Line 283: `has_english = any(["english abstract" in response_lower, ...])`  — same pattern
findings.suggestion: Replace `any([expr1, expr2, ...])` with `any((expr1, expr2, ...))` or simply `expr1 or expr2 or ...` for small fixed sets to enable short-circuit evaluation.

findings.summary: performance.py is a pure re-export shim with a disconnected main() that serves no abstraction purpose
evidence: The entire module body is two import blocks plus __all__ — zero logic

main() at line 47 calls get_performance_monitor() which returns a CodeProfiler, but the --report flag calls monitor.generate_performance_report() on a global singleton that no production call site populates, making the CLI entry point useless in practice
The shim docstring says 'Import from those modules directly for new code' — so the shim's own doc admits callers should bypass it
findings.suggestion: Either delete the shim and migrate call sites to stage_monitor/function_profiler directly, or give it a real aggregating role. Remove or fix the misleading main() entry point.

findings.summary: ReportingStageResult inherits CheckpointStageResult but the added fields (output_files, errors, warnings) are never populated in generate_pipeline_report()
evidence: ReportingStageResult declares output_files, errors, warnings (lines 25-27) as field(default_factory=list)
generate_pipeline_report() constructs each ReportingStageResult at lines 60-67 with only name, exit_code, duration, status — the three new fields are always empty lists
Nothing in the module or its tests ever populates those lists; the inherited extension is dead weight
findings.suggestion: Either populate the fields from stage_results dicts, or drop the inheritance and use a plain dataclass with only the fields that are actually used.

findings.summary: generate_error_summary() silently writes files as a side-effect of what its name and return type imply is a pure aggregation operation
evidence: Function signature returns Dict[str, Any] (the summary), but it also writes error_summary.json and error_summary.md to output_dir (lines 552-559)
The caller receives the dict but has no indication files were written, and no path is returned for the files
By contrast, generate_validation_report() correctly returns Dict[str, Path]; generate_error_summary() has inconsistent API shape
findings.suggestion: Either return Dict[str, Path] like generate_validation_report, or separate the aggregation logic (pure) from the file-writing logic (impure) into two functions.

findings.summary: StageSpec stores func as Callable[[], PipelineStageResult] but all stage methods return bool, creating a type lie
evidence: StageSpec definition at line 68: func: Callable[[], 'PipelineStageResult']
All stage methods (_run_setup_environment, _run_infrastructure_tests, etc.) return bool
_execute_stage at line 244 calls stage_func() and assigns result to 'success', treating it as bool
findings.suggestion: Fix the type annotation on StageSpec.func to Callable[[], bool] to match actual usage.

findings.summary: ProgressBar constructor takes 6 parameters including ETA strategy flags that leak implementation details into the constructor
evidence: ProgressBar.__init__ signature (lines 39-47): total, task, width, show_eta, update_interval, use_ema — 6 parameters
use_ema and show_eta are behavioral policy switches exposed at construction time rather than as strategy objects
SubStageProgress also takes a use_ema flag at line 258, duplicating the same leak
findings.suggestion: Consolidate ETA strategy into a single enum or strategy object; remove duplicated boolean flags across ProgressBar and SubStageProgress.

findings.summary: test_resume_pipeline_missing_checkpoint_runs_fresh only tests the no-checkpoint branch; the valid-checkpoint resume path with partial stages has no test
evidence: test_resume_pipeline_missing_checkpoint_runs_fresh (line 358) calls _resume_pipeline with no checkpoint file present — only exercises the 'start fresh' fallback
The main resume logic (lines 305-365 of pipeline.py) that loads a checkpoint, matches stage names, and continues from a midpoint has zero test coverage
The 'checkpoint stages did not match configured pipeline stages' warning branch at line 344 is also untested
findings.suggestion: Add tests that pre-populate a valid checkpoint via CheckpointManager.save_checkpoint() and verify that execute_full_pipeline() with resume=True skips completed stages and runs only the remaining ones.

findings.summary: test_performance_context_context_manager asserts that stop() was NOT called after the context block, which tests the wrong invariant
evidence: Line 269: 'assert monitor.start_time is not None' with comment 'Context manager logs timing but does not call stop()'
performance_context() actually DOES call stop() internally (stage_monitor.py lines 157-161) — the assertion passes only because start_time is never reset to None after stop()
The test does not verify monitor.last_metrics is set, which is the actual observable postcondition of the context manager
findings.suggestion: Assert monitor.last_metrics is not None after the with-block to test the real postcondition; remove the misleading start_time assertion.

findings.summary: test_performance.py and test_performance_monitor.py both test CodeProfiler with near-identical scenarios, giving no additional coverage
evidence: test_performance.py TestCodeProfiler.test_monitor_appends_to_history (line 511) and test_performance_monitor.py TestPerformanceMonitor.test_monitor_records_metrics (line 85) both verify that a single with profiler.monitor() call appends one entry with the correct operation_name
test_performance.py test_monitor_multiple_entries_appended (line 523) and test_performance_monitor.py test_monitor_multiple_operations (line 99) both verify multiple entries in history
benchmark_function is tested in both files (test_performance.py line 560, test_performance_monitor.py line 137) with trivially different functions
findings.suggestion: Merge the two files into one. Deduplicate tests and use the freed test budget to cover untested paths: profile_function(), get_recent_metrics(), generate_performance_report() with data, and the memory_delta field.

findings.summary: The exit-code-2 graceful-skip path (_as_skip_success) is not covered by any test in test_pipeline.py
evidence: _run_stage_and_checkpoint normalizes exit code 2 to success via _as_skip_success (pipeline.py lines 173-174)
No test in test_pipeline.py constructs a stage function that returns exit code 2 or exercises this branch
test_skip_llm_execution verifies LLM scripts are not invoked when skip_llm=True, but this uses stage skipping (skip=True on StageSpec), not the exit-code-2 path
findings.suggestion: Add a test where a script exits with code 2 and allow_skip_code=True; verify the stage result has success=True and exit_code=2.

findings.summary: save_pipeline_report's OSError handling (raises on write failure) is not tested
evidence: Lines 127-134, 139-146, 148-158 of pipeline_reporter.py catch OSError and re-raise
The test file only creates reports in valid tmp_path directories — no test attempts to write to a read-only path or otherwise triggers the error branch
The 'project_name with output_statistics' enrichment branch (lines 70-79) is also not covered by any test in test_pipeline_reporter.py
findings.suggestion: Add tests for: (1) save_pipeline_report with an unwritable output_dir raises OSError; (2) generate_pipeline_report with project_name + output_statistics enriches the output_statistics dict with log_file info.

findings.summary: logging_utils.py is simultaneously a logger factory, a context manager library, a decorator library, a progress adapter, and a resource monitor — too many responsibilities in one module
evidence: Module exports 18 public names in __all__ spanning: logger config (setup_logger, get_logger), context managers (log_operation, log_timing), decorators (log_function_call, monitor_performance), formatting helpers (format_duration, format_error_with_suggestions), progress helpers (log_stage, log_stage_with_eta, log_progress, log_substep), and resource monitoring (log_resource_usage)
The module already re-imports from four split submodules (logging_formatters, logging_helpers, logging_progress, logging_constants) indicating a decomposition started but not completed
log_resource_usage at line 386 duplicates psutil process inspection already in stage_monitor.py
findings.suggestion: Complete the decomposition: move decorator/context manager utilities to logging_ops.py; move the stage/progress log helpers to logging_progress.py (they are already there in part); remove log_resource_usage and point callers to stage_monitor.

findings.summary: generate_html_report() assembles HTML by f-string concatenation scattered across the function body rather than a template, making the boundary between logic and markup invisible
evidence: Lines 286-399: a multi-hundred-line f-string literal for the static frame, followed by a loop (lines 401-410) appending per-stage rows via html +=, then conditional section blocks appended at lines 418-431
The function is 165 lines and mixes control flow with HTML generation throughout
generate_markdown_report() by contrast uses a clean lines.append() list pattern with a single join at the end — the two sibling functions have completely different structural styles
findings.suggestion: Extract the HTML skeleton to a Jinja2 or string.Template template, or at minimum adopt the same lines-list pattern used in generate_markdown_report() for consistency.

findings.summary: _start_fresh() mutates self.config.resume to avoid infinite recursion, which is a fragile state-machine workaround for a design flaw
evidence: _start_fresh() at lines 281-290 saves config.resume, sets it to False, calls execute_full_pipeline() or execute_core_pipeline(), then restores it in finally
This pattern is needed because execute_full_pipeline() checks self.config.resume to route to _resume_pipeline(), which calls _start_fresh(), creating a potential recursion

PipelineConfig is a dataclass with no protection against mutation; a concurrent call or an exception during execute_full_pipeline() could leave config.resume=False permanently if the finally block is somehow skipped
findings.suggestion: Pass a resume=False flag explicitly to an internal _execute_fresh() helper rather than mutating shared config state; or add a private _resuming flag to the executor itself.

findings.summary: StagePerformanceTracker.end_stage() records current memory as both memory_mb and peak_memory_mb, so peak is always equal to current and is never actually tracked as a peak
evidence: Lines 235-237: current_memory = process.memory_info().rss / 1024 / 1024; metrics['memory_mb'] = current_memory; metrics['peak_memory_mb'] = current_memory
There is no sampling loop or high-water mark tracking between start_stage and end_stage; the value is a single snapshot at end time, not a peak
start_stage() captures self.start_memory but end_stage() never uses it — the delta and the true peak are both lost
findings.suggestion: Either remove peak_memory_mb from end_stage() output (since it cannot be computed from a single snapshot) or implement periodic sampling, or rename the field to end_memory_mb to accurately describe what is measured.

findings.summary: get_logger() may silently rebuild and clear handlers on every call in test environments, making logger identity unstable
evidence: Lines 200-203: if in test env and not logger.propagate: logger.handlers.clear(); return setup_logger(name) — this clears and rebuilds handlers each time get_logger is called on a non-propagating logger in test
setup_logger() itself always calls logger.handlers.clear() (line 85) before adding handlers, so get_logger → setup_logger is always destructive
Any file handler attached by PipelineExecutor._setup_log_file_handler via setup_logger(__name__) would be silently removed by a subsequent get_logger(__name__) call in a test context
findings.suggestion: Separate test-environment reconfiguration into an explicit setup_for_test() call invoked from conftest.py; make get_logger() truly idempotent by never clearing handlers.

findings.summary: LLMProgressTracker._display() computes elapsed with time.time() - self.start_time and immediately discards it
evidence: Line 200: 'time.time() - self.start_time' is a bare expression with no assignment — the result is computed and thrown away
The variable is needed for the ETA branch below (line 213 uses throughput which was computed from elapsed in update_tokens), but elapsed itself is not reused in _display()
findings.suggestion: Remove the dead expression on line 200, or assign it to elapsed and use it explicitly to make the intent clear.
